svg.title: Retrieval, two lanes. Build-time: each chunk goes down a dense lane (embedding model to a vector in a vector store) and a sparse lane (analyzer to terms in an inverted index). Query-time mirrors it: the question is embedded to a query vector for the dense lane and analyzed to query terms for the sparse lane, each searched against its own store; the invariant is that the query uses the same transform as the index. Top-K matched chunks converge, their text and metadata build the prompt, the LLM answers. The search-store slot is swappable: dense, sparse, hybrid (fused with RRF), or hybrid plus a cross-encoder re-ranker. Color codes mechanism: teal is dense vectors, amber is sparse inverted index, purple is the shared prompt and LLM tail.
svg.text: Retrieval — two lanes, two mechanisms, one shared tail
Dense = vectors, matched by similarity. Sparse = an inverted index, matched by terms. Both are built, then queried the same way.
① Build-time — turn each chunk into something searchable (the two lanes run in parallel)
Same chunk is stored twice — once as a
vector, once as terms. They are different
machines, not stages of one pipeline.
★ The invariant: at query time each lane must use the same transform as build — the same embedding model (dense) and the same analyzer (sparse).
If they differ — index stems "shipped"→"ship" but the query doesn't — you get zero results, no error. This is the classic search bug.
② Query-time — the same two lanes, mirrored: dense embeds the question, sparse analyzes it
★ You search by vector or by terms, but you always pass the chunk's TEXT + metadata to the LLM — the vector / terms were only the way to find it.
★ The LLM only sees what you retrieved — retrieval quality is the ceiling on answer quality.
③ Candidate generation is the swappable choice — dense, sparse, or hybrid. Re-rank then layers on top of any of them.
inverted index: word → chunks containing it
BM25 ranks by how often & how rare a term is
good for: names, codes, IDs, exact phrases
run BOTH lanes, fuse the lists
RRF blends each item's rank in both lists
+ Re-rank (optional) — layers on ANY of the above:

A cross-encoder re-reads (query + chunk) together and re-orders the top few. Lane-agnostic — it doesn't care how candidates were found. Higher precision, but slower & pricier → re-rank only the top handful.
④ Strategies to make retrieval better — four stages (next week's topic)
Start with semantic top-K; hybrid (often + a re-ranker) is the common production choice.
Each added layer costs latency & money — add it because a real miss demands it, not by default.
